content.text: Why Exercises Are Separate from Documentation

A DR plan that has never been tested is a hypothesis. Tabletop exercises and live drills convert the hypothesis into evidence. Teams that skip exercises consistently discover the same categories of failure during real incidents: contacts embedded in wikis rather than runbooks (requiring a search during a crisis), fine-tuned models not synced to the DR region, kill-switch procedures that no one has authority to execute, and failback procedures that cause a second outage.

GenAI services have a distinct set of failure modes that standard BCP exercises do not cover: safety classifier outages, model version mismatches between regions, provider-level API failures that are outside the team's control, and GPU availability constraints in the DR region. Exercises must be designed to surface these specifically.

attrs.alt: BCP exercise and DR drill program: four types of exercises from tabletop to chaos injection, scenario flow, and common GenAI findings

content.text: Exercises form a fidelity spectrum. Start with tabletop exercises to build shared understanding, then progress to functional and full-scale drills as the team matures and the runbooks stabilize:
Reveals hidden assumptions, tests team readiness

Many teams skip straight to live drills because they seem more rigorous. In practice, tabletop exercises surface procedural gaps — who has authority, which contacts to call, what the escalation path is — that are cheaper to find in a meeting room than during a live failover.

A tabletop scenario has three components: a trigger (the initial inject that starts the exercise), two or three complicating injects that escalate the scenario, and a set of learning objectives that the facilitator checks at the end. GenAI tabletops must include at least one of:
A provider-level outage (not just a pod failure) — requires the team to navigate a vendor support channel while also managing the incident
A safety classifier degradation — forces an explicit decision about whether to block completions or serve degraded-filtered output
A fine-tuned model unavailability in the DR region — reveals whether the model sync audit exists and whether a base-model fallback is defined
A simultaneous outage in both the primary inference tier and a dependency (e.g., vector index down during provider failover) — tests that the team does not assume only one thing can fail at a time

# Tabletop scenario library: structured injects for a 60-min session
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List

@dataclass
class Inject:
    time_min: int
    description: str
    discussion_prompts: List[str]

@dataclass
class TabletopScenario:
    title: str
    trigger: str           # what the facilitator reads at T=0
    injects: List[Inject]
    learning_objectives: List[str]

# Scenario 1: LLM provider outage + safety classifier degraded
SCENARIO_PROVIDER_OUTAGE = TabletopScenario(
    title='Primary LLM Provider Outage with Safety Degradation',
    trigger=(
        'It is 14:30 on a Tuesday. The PagerDuty alert fires: '
        'llm_requests_error_rate > 20% for model gpt4-class in us-east-1. '
        'The Anthropic status page shows a partial outage in us-east-1.'
    ),
    injects=[
        Inject(
            time_min=10,
            description=(
                'The fallback provider (Bedrock) is taking 3x normal latency. '
                'P95 TTFT is now 12s. Your SLO threshold is 4s.'
            ),
            discussion_prompts=[
                'Do you activate rate limiting? Which tenants get deprioritized?',
                'Who communicates to customers? What is the message?',
                'Do you open the provider support ticket now or wait?',
            ],
        ),
        Inject(
            time_min=25,
            description=(
                'The safety classifier returns 503 for 40% of requests. '
                'You cannot confirm whether completions are being filtered correctly.'
            ),
            discussion_prompts=[
                'Do you block all completions or allow degraded filtering?',
                'Who has authority to make this decision?',
                'What is the customer communication for a safety-classifier outage?',
            ],
        ),
    ],
    learning_objectives=[
        'Validate escalation chain for simultaneous provider + safety outage',
        'Identify who owns the decision to disable completions',
        'Verify provider TAM contact is in runbook (not just a wiki)',
    ],
)

# Scenario 2: Region failover with fine-tuned model missing in DR
SCENARIO_DR_MODEL_MISSING = TabletopScenario(
    title='Region Failover — Fine-Tuned Model Missing in DR',
    trigger=(
        'It is 02:15 UTC. The watchdog fires: us-east-1 primary region health '
        'checks have failed 3 consecutive times. DNS has flipped to us-west-2. '
        'The on-call SRE is paged.'
    ),
    injects=[
        Inject(
            time_min=12,
            description=(
                'The DR region pods are returning 500 for requests to /v1/chat. '
                'The logs show: ModelNotFound: customer-finetuned-v3 not available '
                'in us-west-2/models/production.'
            ),
            discussion_prompts=[
                'Can you serve requests with the base model? Is that acceptable?',
                'How long will it take to sync the fine-tuned model from us-east-1?',
                'Is us-east-1 accessible enough to sync from, or is it fully down?',
            ],
        ),
    ],
    learning_objectives=[
        'Discover that DR model sync audit is not running',
        'Define the degraded-mode fallback for fine-tuned model outage',
        'Add model sync check to DR readiness criteria',
    ],
)

A 60-minute tabletop session follows a standard structure. The facilitator is not the incident commander — they present the scenario and ask questions. The SRE on-call engineer plays the incident commander role throughout:
share the scenario title and trigger text. Do not share the injects — they should feel like new information.
facilitator reads the trigger. Incident commander declares roles: who is IC, comms lead, subject matter expert.
team walks through the first response. Facilitator asks: 'what exactly would you do? Who calls whom? What command do you run?'
facilitator delivers Injects 2 and 3 as new information arriving during the incident. Team responds in character.
hot wash — what decisions were unclear? What gaps in the runbook surfaced? What contacts were missing?
action items assigned. Each gap becomes a Jira ticket before the meeting ends. No ticket = no accountability.

Functional drills execute the actual failover runbook against a staging environment. The goal is to validate that the automation works — the watchdog fires, the DNS flips, the DR pods start serving — and to measure how long each step takes. Full-scale drills do the same against production:

# DR drill runner: automated measurement of RTO for Tier 1 failover
import time, logging, dataclasses
from typing import List

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclasses.dataclass
class DrillStep:
    name: str
    started_at: float = 0.0
    completed_at: float = 0.0
    passed: bool = False
    notes: str = ''

    @property
    def duration_s(self) -> float:
        return self.completed_at - self.started_at

@dataclasses.dataclass
class DrillReport:
    drill_id: str
    scenario: str
    started_at: float
    steps: List[DrillStep] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=list)
    total_rto_minutes: float = 0.0
    rto_target_minutes: float = 15.0

    @property
    def passed(self) -> bool:
        return (
            all(s.passed for s in self.steps)
            and self.total_rto_minutes <= self.rto_target_minutes
        )

    def summary(self) -> str:
        result = 'PASS' if self.passed else 'FAIL'
        lines = [
            f'DR Drill {self.drill_id} [{self.scenario}]: {result}',
            f'  Total RTO: {self.total_rto_minutes:.1f} min (target: {self.rto_target_minutes} min)',
        ]
        for step in self.steps:
            ok = 'OK' if step.passed else 'FAIL'
            lines.append(f'  [{ok}] {step.name}: {step.duration_s:.0f}s')
            if step.notes:
                lines.append(f'       -> {step.notes}')
        return chr(10).join(lines)

def run_tier1_failover_drill(drill_id: str) -> DrillReport:
    report = DrillReport(
        drill_id=drill_id,
        scenario='Tier1-RegionFailover',
        started_at=time.time(),
    )
    steps = [
        'Block primary health check endpoint',
        'Verify watchdog detects failure within 90s',
        'Confirm DNS flipped to DR (dig api.genai.internal)',
        'Verify DR region serving 200s (/health + /v1/models)',
        'Confirm error rate < 1% on DR region',
        'Restore primary health check endpoint',
        'Verify primary is healthy for 5 consecutive minutes',
        'Gradual failback: shift 10% -> 50% -> 100% to primary',
    ]
    for step_name in steps:
        step = DrillStep(name=step_name, started_at=time.time())
        report.steps.append(step)
        # In real drill: execute the step and record the outcome
        # step.passed = execute_step(step_name)
        step.completed_at = time.time()
    report.total_rto_minutes = (time.time() - report.started_at) / 60
    log.info(report.summary())
    return report

The drill runner records wall-clock time for each step. The total RTO is compared against the target (15 minutes for Tier 1). If any step exceeds its time budget or fails, the drill is recorded as a FAIL and the specific step becomes a Jira remediation item. A full-scale drill that takes 22 minutes when the target is 15 is more valuable than a passing grade — it proves where the time is actually spent.
Schedule drills on low-traffic windows but not at 3am.

Drills should reflect realistic operating conditions. Running a drill at 3am on a Sunday when traffic is minimal does not validate that the system handles real load during failover. Run drills during business hours at moderate traffic levels.

Every exercise produces a set of findings — gaps in the runbook, missing contacts, automation that didn't work, or decisions that couldn't be made without escalation. Each finding becomes a Jira ticket that is tracked through closure before the next exercise of the same type:

# Post-exercise action item tracker: creates Jira tickets from findings
import json, os, urllib.request, urllib.parse
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class ExerciseFinding:
    title: str
    description: str
    severity: str       # 'critical' | 'high' | 'medium' | 'low'
    owner_team: str
    due_sprint: str     # e.g. '2026-Q3-S1'

# Standard findings from GenAI DR exercises
COMMON_GENAI_FINDINGS: List[ExerciseFinding] = [
    ExerciseFinding(
        title='Safety classifier kill-switch not in runbook',
        description=(
            'During tabletop, team could not agree on whether to block completions '
            'when safety classifier is unavailable. Add explicit kill-switch step.'
        ),
        severity='critical',
        owner_team='ml-platform',
        due_sprint='current',
    ),
    ExerciseFinding(
        title='Provider TAM phone number not in runbook',
        description=(
            'On-call engineer had to search the wiki during simulated outage. '
            'Embed contact directly in runbook preamble.'
        ),
        severity='high',
        owner_team='sre',
        due_sprint='current',
    ),
    ExerciseFinding(
        title='DR model sync audit not automated',
        description=(
            'Full-scale drill revealed fine-tuned adapter missing in DR region. '
            'Add automated weekly audit with PagerDuty alert on missing models.'
        ),
        severity='critical',
        owner_team='infra',
        due_sprint='next',
    ),
]

def create_jira_from_finding(finding: ExerciseFinding, jira_base: str, token: str) -> str:
    priority_map = {'critical': 'Highest', 'high': 'High',
                    'medium': 'Medium', 'low': 'Low'}
    body = {
        'fields': {
            'project': {'key': 'SRE'},
            'summary': f'[DR Exercise] {finding.title}',
            'description': {'type': 'doc', 'version': 1, 'content': [
                {'type': 'paragraph', 'content': [
                    {'type': 'text', 'text': finding.description}]}
            ]},
            'issuetype': {'name': 'Task'},
            'priority': {'name': priority_map[finding.severity]},
            'labels': ['dr-exercise', 'bcp', finding.owner_team],
        }
    }
    creds = urllib.parse.quote(f'sre-bot:{token}')
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        f'{jira_base}/rest/api/3/issue',
        data=json.dumps(body).encode(),
        headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json',
                 'Authorization': f'Basic {creds}'},
        method='POST',
    )
    return json.loads(urllib.request.urlopen(req).read())['key']
SRE lead reviews at weekly meeting

Chaos engineering — injecting failures into production without warning — is the highest-fidelity test of system and team resilience. It is appropriate only for teams that have completed at least two full-scale drills with passing grades and whose runbooks are fully verified. The value of unannounced chaos is that it reveals whether the team can respond to alerts they were not expecting, not just alerts they were scheduled to practice:

# Chaos injection for GenAI services: controlled failure scenarios
# Only run with change management approval and on-call engineer available.
import subprocess, time, logging

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

NAMESPACE = 'genai-prod'

def inject_pod_failure(deployment: str, replica_count: int = 1):
    """Delete N pods from a deployment to simulate pod crashes."""
    result = subprocess.run([
        'kubectl', '-n', NAMESPACE, 'get', 'pods',
        '-l', f'app={deployment}',
        '-o', 'jsonpath={.items[*].metadata.name}',
    ], capture_output=True, text=True)
    pods = result.stdout.split()
    targets = pods[:replica_count]
    for pod in targets:
        subprocess.run(
            ['kubectl', '-n', NAMESPACE, 'delete', 'pod', pod],
            check=True,
        )
        log.warning('[CHAOS] Deleted pod %s', pod)

def inject_network_latency(service: str, latency_ms: int = 500, duration_s: int = 120):
    """Add artificial latency to a service via tc netem (requires privileged pods)."""
    # This is an illustrative pattern -- actual implementation uses
    # Chaos Mesh or Litmus Chaos for Kubernetes-native injection.
    log.warning('[CHAOS] Injecting %dms latency to %s for %ds', latency_ms, service, duration_s)
    # chaos_mesh.NetworkChaos(target=service, latency=f'{latency_ms}ms', duration=f'{duration_s}s')

def inject_gpu_memory_pressure(deployment: str):
    """Send a large batch of requests to exhaust KV cache (load-based chaos)."""
    # Use a locust / k6 load profile that targets the KV-cache limit
    log.warning('[CHAOS] Injecting GPU memory pressure on %s', deployment)
    # subprocess.run(['k6', 'run', 'chaos/gpu_pressure.js', '--env', f'TARGET={deployment}'])

SAFE_CHAOS_SCENARIOS = [
    ('Pod crash - 1 of 4 replicas', inject_pod_failure, {'deployment': 'llm-server', 'replica_count': 1}),
    ('Network latency 500ms', inject_network_latency, {'service': 'llm-server', 'latency_ms': 500}),
    ('Provider circuit open', None, 'Block primary provider health check via iptables'),
]
For GenAI services, the highest-value chaos scenarios target the failure modes that conventional chaos engineering misses:
block the primary LLM API endpoint at the network level. Verify the circuit-breaker opens and routes to the fallback within the configured failure threshold.
send a burst of long-context requests that fills the KV-cache. Verify the model server queues and rejects gracefully rather than crashing.
add 5-second latency to the classifier endpoint. Verify that the inference server returns a timeout rather than bypassing the safety check.
deploy a stub that returns an incorrect model version header. Verify that monitoring detects the mismatch before customers report quality degradation.
The exercise calendar ensures that the four types of exercises happen at the right cadence and that findings from each exercise are resolved before the next one:
DR sync audit review (not an exercise)

BCP tabletop exercises and DR drills are distinct activities that together validate the full DR plan. Tabletop exercises surface procedural gaps (missing contacts, unclear authority, undefined degraded modes) with zero risk and low cost. Functional drills validate automation in staging. Full-scale drills measure actual RTO against target in production. Chaos injection tests team resilience under unannounced failure conditions. GenAI-specific scenarios must cover provider outages, safety classifier degradation, and DR model sync failures. Every exercise produces Jira tickets; findings are graded by severity with time-bound resolution targets, and no exercise type is repeated until the prior round's critical findings are closed.
